skill.name: medchem
skill.description: Medicinal chemistry guidance for applying drug-likeness rules, PAINS filters, structural alerts, molecular complexity metrics, and batch compound filtering workflows.
skill.summary: Apply medicinal chemistry filters and prioritize compound libraries with medchem workflows.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution and high-risk blocker matches, but review found they are Markdown examples and medicinal chemistry terminology. The only confirmed operational risk is an optional script that reads local compound files and writes user-selected output files. No prompt injection, credential access, network exfiltration, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Optional script reads and writes local molecule files
low_findings.description: The batch script loads user-provided CSV, TSV, SDF, or text files and writes CSV and summary output paths chosen by the user. This is expected for compound filtering, but users should run it only on trusted inputs and intentional output paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly performs local file loading and output writing. No evidence shows automatic execution, hidden paths, credential access, or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static command execution matches are Markdown examples
low_findings.description: The external command detections are caused by Markdown code fences and inline Python or Bash examples. They document how to install medchem and call APIs; no hidden command execution logic was found in the skill instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are fenced documentation examples, not executable Ruby backticks or shell invocation code. Search found no subprocess, os.system, eval, or exec usage.

low_findings.title: Static malware and weak-crypto matches are chemistry terminology
low_findings.description: High-severity blocker matches map to medicinal chemistry terms such as aldehydes, acid halides, epoxides, acrylamides, and scaffold examples. These are domain terms and molecular patterns, not cryptography, C2, or malware behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text is a medicinal chemistry catalog and skill description. No encoded payloads, remote endpoints, persistence logic, or command-and-control behavior were found.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are documentation links
low_findings.description: The network findings point to official documentation and repository URLs in the Resources section. They are plain text references and are not used by executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are listed under Documentation and are not fetched by the skill or script. Search found no network client imports or outbound request code.

content.value_statement: Compound triage is slow when rules, alerts, and assay-interference checks are scattered. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured medchem workflows for prioritizing molecules.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to apply Lipinski, Veber, CNS, REOS, lead-like, and fragment rules.
Guides PAINS, NIBR, Lilly demerits, and common structural alert screening.
Supports batch filtering workflows for CSV, TSV, SDF, and SMILES text inputs.
Helps combine rule results, alert results, complexity metrics, and custom constraints.
Provides reference material for medchem modules, functions, parameters, and return formats.
Shows how to detect chemical groups such as hinge binders, phosphate binders, and reactive groups.
limitations: It depends on the external medchem, datamol, pandas, tqdm, and RDKit Python packages.
Rules are screening heuristics and do not prove safety, efficacy, or developability.
The script writes local output files and should be run with deliberate paths.
It does not replace expert medicinal chemistry review or experimental validation.

use_cases.title: Prioritize screening libraries
use_cases.description: Apply drug-likeness rules and structural alerts to reduce large compound sets before assay planning.

use_cases.title: Review lead optimization candidates
use_cases.description: Compare candidate molecules against lead-like rules, Lilly demerits, NIBR filters, and molecular complexity limits.

use_cases.title: Prepare reproducible filtering reports
use_cases.description: Use the batch script and references to create repeatable CSV outputs and summary reports for compound decisions.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a basic filter set
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a small-molecule screening library. Recommend a basic medchem filter set and explain what each filter catches.

prompt_templates.title: Interpret filtering results
prompt_templates.prompt: These compounds passed Rule of Five but failed PAINS or common alerts. Help me interpret the result and decide next checks.

prompt_templates.title: Design a batch workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a medchem batch filtering workflow for a CSV with a smiles column, NIBR alerts, PAINS, complexity, and an output summary.

prompt_templates.title: Build a staged prioritization strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a staged prioritization strategy that combines lead-like rules, Lilly demerits, target context, complexity, and exception handling.

output_examples.input: Ask for first-pass filters for a screening library.
output_examples.output: A concise filter plan covering Rule of Five, Veber, PAINS, common alerts, and when to review exceptions manually.

output_examples.input: Ask how to process a CSV of SMILES with NIBR and complexity checks.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step batch workflow that identifies input requirements, selected filters, expected output columns, and review checkpoints.

output_examples.input: Ask whether a reactive group should always reject a candidate.
output_examples.output: A balanced explanation that distinguishes unwanted liabilities from intentional covalent warheads and recommends expert review.

best_practices: Use filters as decision support, not automatic rejection rules.
Document which rules were applied and why each compound was removed.
Combine structural alerts with target class, modality, route, and assay context.
anti_patterns: Reject every molecule that violates one rule without context.
Compare libraries without recording medchem package versions and filter settings.
Run the batch script on untrusted files or accidental output paths.

faq.question: Does this skill install medchem automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents installation and usage. Users must install required Python packages in their own environment.

faq.question: Can it prove a compound is a good drug candidate?
faq.answer: No. It helps prioritize molecules with screening rules, but experimental and expert review remain necessary.

faq.question: What input formats does the script support?
faq.answer: The script supports CSV, TSV, SDF, and plain text SMILES files.

faq.question: Does it make network requests?
faq.answer: No evidence of runtime network requests was found. The URLs in the skill are documentation links.

faq.question: Why are PAINS filters included?
faq.answer: PAINS filters help flag compounds that may interfere with assays and create misleading activity signals.

faq.question: Should failed filters always remove a molecule?
faq.answer: No. Some valid drugs and special modalities fail common rules, so context and expert judgment are required.
